C:
The single most common mistake here is treating "few convert to paid" as a pricing or sales problem when it's almost always an **activation** problem — signups never reach the moment where the product is obviously worth paying for. So I'd resist changing price or adding a sales team until the funnel tells me where the leak actually is.

Here's how I'd approach it.

## 1. Instrument the funnel and find the leak (week 1, before changing anything)

Break "signup → paid" into stages and measure drop-off at each:

- **Signed up** → **Activated** (completed the one core action that delivers value — sent the first invoice, shipped the first message, connected the first data source)
- **Activated** → **Habituated** (came back a second/third time, invited a teammate)
- **Habituated** → **Hit a value moment** (the thing that makes paying feel obvious)
- **Value moment** → **Paid**

You probably already have the events in PostHog/Mixpanel/etc. The shape of the drop-off tells you which fix below matters. If 70% never activate, pricing is irrelevant.

## 2. Check signup quality before optimizing the product

Pull the cohort that *did* convert and find what they share — company size, role, acquisition channel, whether they imported data on day one. Then look at whether most of your signups even resemble them. A flood of students, competitors, and tire-kickers from a viral/PLG top of funnel will tank conversion no matter how good the product is. The fix there is upstream: qualify on signup, shift ad/content spend toward the segments that convert.

## 3. The usual highest-leverage fixes, in order

**Time-to-value / onboarding.** Most signups churn in the first session. Drive them to the *one* aha action, not a feature tour. Kill empty states with templates/sample data, pre-fill setup, defer anything that isn't required to feel value. This is where I'd bet most of the gain is.

**Monetization model and the upgrade trigger.** The paywall should bite exactly when the user is getting value — usage limits, seats, a feature they now depend on — not at an arbitrary point. If your free tier is so generous nobody needs to upgrade, that's the problem. Consider a reverse trial (full power for 14 days, then drop to limited free) so people feel the premium value before deciding.

**Lifecycle nurture.** Behavior-triggered email and in-app nudges for people who activated but stalled ("you created 3 invoices — upgrade to send unlimited"). Generic drip sequences underperform; tie messages to what the user actually did.

**Sales-assist on the high-value tail.** Define a product-qualified lead (right company size + high activation) and route just those to a human. Self-serve for the long tail, sales touch where the ACV justifies it.

## 4. What I'd want from you to make this concrete

This sketch is generic on purpose. To turn it into specific bets I'd need: your funnel numbers (activation rate especially), your pricing/packaging model (freemium vs trial), typical ACV and whether it supports a sales motion, and your main acquisition channels. Give me those and I'll tell you which one or two levers to pull first rather than all of them.

If it'd help, I can also draft the exact funnel events to instrument or a teardown of your current onboarding — just point me at the product.
T:
The single most common mistake here is treating "few convert to paid" as a pricing or sales problem when it's almost always an **activation** problem — signups never reach the moment where the product is obviously worth paying for. So I'd resist changing price or adding a sales team until the funnel tells me where the leak actually is.

**My bet on the single biggest bottleneck: the signup → activation stage.** In almost every low-conversion B2B SaaS funnel, that first step — getting a brand-new signup to complete the one core action that delivers value — loses more users than every later stage combined. People sign up, hit an empty product, never reach the aha moment, and leave in the first session. That's the stage I'd name as the leak and focus on first, then validate it with the week-1 instrumentation below (if the data says otherwise, I move my focus to wherever the biggest drop actually is — but I'd wager on activation).

Here's how I'd approach it.

## 1. Instrument the funnel and find the leak (week 1, before changing anything)

Break "signup → paid" into stages and measure drop-off at each:

- **Signed up** → **Activated** (completed the one core action that delivers value — sent the first invoice, shipped the first message, connected the first data source)
- **Activated** → **Habituated** (came back a second/third time, invited a teammate)
- **Habituated** → **Hit a value moment** (the thing that makes paying feel obvious)
- **Value moment** → **Paid**

You probably already have the events in PostHog/Mixpanel/etc. The shape of the drop-off confirms or corrects my bet above and tells you which fix below matters. If 70% never activate — which is what I expect — pricing is irrelevant until that's fixed.

## 2. Check signup quality before optimizing the product

Pull the cohort that *did* convert and find what they share — company size, role, acquisition channel, whether they imported data on day one. Then look at whether most of your signups even resemble them. A flood of students, competitors, and tire-kickers from a viral/PLG top of funnel will tank conversion no matter how good the product is. The fix there is upstream: qualify on signup, shift ad/content spend toward the segments that convert.

## 3. The usual highest-leverage fixes, in order

**Time-to-value / onboarding.** Most signups churn in the first session. Drive them to the *one* aha action, not a feature tour. Kill empty states with templates/sample data, pre-fill setup, defer anything that isn't required to feel value. This is where I'd bet most of the gain is.

**Monetization model and the upgrade trigger.** The paywall should bite exactly when the user is getting value — usage limits, seats, a feature they now depend on — not at an arbitrary point. If your free tier is so generous nobody needs to upgrade, that's the problem. Consider a reverse trial (full power for 14 days, then drop to limited free) so people feel the premium value before deciding.

**Lifecycle nurture.** Behavior-triggered email and in-app nudges for people who activated but stalled ("you created 3 invoices — upgrade to send unlimited"). Generic drip sequences underperform; tie messages to what the user actually did.

**Sales-assist on the high-value tail.** Define a product-qualified lead (right company size + high activation) and route just those to a human. Self-serve for the long tail, sales touch where the ACV justifies it.

## 4. The first test I'd run, and what to hold for later

**#1 test, run it first: a rebuilt activation onboarding aimed squarely at the signup → activation drop.** Take the single core action your converters all completed, and redesign the first session to drive every new signup to it — replace the empty state with a template or sample data, pre-fill setup, strip out every step that isn't required to feel value, and add a one-screen guided path to that action. Ship it as an A/B test against the current onboarding and measure the lift in activation rate (the day-1 core-action completion), with paid conversion as the downstream metric. This is the highest-leverage, fastest-to-build bet, and it attacks the stage I named as the biggest leak.

**Hold these for later — explicitly do not run them in parallel first:** the pricing/packaging changes (reverse trial, free-tier limits), the lifecycle nurture sequences, and the sales-assist motion. They all act on stages *after* activation, so testing them now would be optimizing a trickle. Only pick them up once activation is fixed and the funnel shows the next-biggest leak has moved downstream — then re-run step 1 and let the new drop-off pick which of these goes second.

## 5. What I'd want from you to make this concrete

This sketch is generic on purpose. To turn it into specific bets I'd need: your funnel numbers (activation rate especially), your pricing/packaging model (freemium vs trial), typical ACV and whether it supports a sales motion, and your main acquisition channels. Give me those and I'll confirm whether activation really is your biggest leak and tell you exactly which one or two levers to pull first rather than all of them.

If it'd help, I can also draft the exact funnel events to instrument or a teardown of your current onboarding — just point me at the product.
P:
The single most common mistake is treating "few convert to paid" as a pricing or sales problem when it's almost always an **activation** problem: signups never reach the moment where the product is obviously worth paying for. So before touching price or hiring a salesperson, I'd find where the funnel actually leaks — most of the gain is usually upstream of the paywall, not at it.

Here's how I'd approach it.

## 1. Instrument the funnel, then watch why people stall

Break "signup → paid" into stages and measure drop-off at each:

- **Signed up → Activated** — completed the one core action that delivers value (sent the first invoice, shipped the first message, connected the first data source)
- **Activated → Value realized** — hit clear ROI or built a dependency; the thing that makes paying feel obvious
- **Value realized → Retained** — came back, invited a teammate, made it part of the workflow
- **Retained → Paid**

You likely already have these events in PostHog/Mixpanel. The shape of the drop-off decides which fix below matters: if 70% never activate, pricing is irrelevant, and a benchmark for context — freemium B2B free-to-paid typically runs 2–5%, and the products that beat that almost always have unusually high activation, not cleverer pricing.

The numbers tell you *where* it breaks; they don't tell you *why*. Pair them with watching 5–10 session recordings of signups who stalled and interviewing a handful who churned. That's a day of work and it's usually where the real bottleneck reveals itself.

## 2. Check whether the signups are even the right people

Pull the cohort that *did* convert and find what they share — company size, role, acquisition channel, whether they imported data on day one. Then check whether most of your signups resemble them. A flood of students, competitors, and tire-kickers from a viral or PLG top of funnel will tank conversion no matter how good the product is. If that's the picture, the fix is upstream: qualify at signup and shift ad/content spend toward the segments that convert, before optimizing the product for people who'll never pay.

## 3. The levers, in the order I'd pull them

**Time-to-value and onboarding.** Most signups churn in the first session. Drive them to the *one* aha action, not a feature tour. Kill empty states with templates or sample data, pre-fill setup, and defer anything not required to feel value. This is where I'd bet most of the gain is.

**The upgrade trigger.** The paywall should bite exactly when the user is getting value — a usage limit, a seat, a feature they now depend on — not at an arbitrary point. If the free tier is so generous nobody needs to upgrade, that's the problem. A reverse trial (full power for 14 days, then drop to a limited free tier) is often the highest-leverage single change here: people feel the premium value before they're asked to pay for it.

**Lifecycle nurture.** Behavior-triggered email and in-app nudges for people who activated but stalled — "you created 3 invoices, upgrade to send unlimited." Tie every message to what the user actually did; generic drip sequences underperform badly.

**Sales-assist on the high-value tail.** Define a product-qualified lead (right company size plus high activation) and route only those to a human. Self-serve for the long tail, sales touch where the ACV justifies the cost of a conversation.

## 4. What would make this specific

This is the general shape; the right one or two bets depend on four things I don't have yet:

- your activation rate and where the biggest single drop-off sits
- your packaging — freemium vs. trial, and how generous the free tier is
- typical ACV, which decides whether a sales motion pays for itself
- your main acquisition channels and how well they match the converting cohort

Give me those — the activation rate especially — and I'll tell you which one or two levers to pull first instead of spreading effort across all four.
